FT_IC1.evidence: Uses baseline data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study collected across 21 study sites in the United States. N=10,712 children aged 9-10 years.

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time is part of a 24-h movement behavior composite outcome (physical activity, screen time, sleep met/not met). Only composite outcome results are reported; screen-time-specific coefficients are not separately extractable. Per E2: 'Inside 24-h movement composite — phone/SM-specific row/column extractable → MET. Whole-composite only → NOT_MET.'

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression conducted analyzing associations between family typologies (predictors) and movement behavior recommendations met (outcome), with OR values reported for composite outcome.

FT_IC4.evidence: OR and 95% CI values reported in Table 3: e.g., for ≥1 recommendations met: Age (OR=0.71, 95% CI 0.66-0.77); Family income (OR=1.14, 95% CI 1.12-1.16); Sex (OR=1.20, 95% CI 1.10-1.31)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis from ABCD baseline dataset using latent profile analysis and logistic regression

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed journal article (International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 2021)

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes cross-sectional analysis. Although screen time is part of the movement behavior outcome (as DV) and predictors include demographic/family variables, the cross-sectional design means FT-EC7 does not apply per E7: 'cross-sectional any predictor → MET; longitudinal demographic-only → FT-EC7 applies'
notes: Screen time measured but only as part of composite 24-h movement behavior outcome. FT-IC2 NOT_MET because no screen-time-specific coefficient is extractable (E2).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study with 9,718 school-age children ages 9-11

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper identifies 'screen media activity' as one of the key factors associated with fluid cognitive functioning

FT_IC3.evidence: Machine learning framework used to identify factors associated with fluid cognitive functioning, with screen media activity identified as a significant contributor

FT_IC4.evidence: Algorithm explained 14.74% of the variance in fluid cognitive functioning, indicating quantifiable contribution of identified factors including screen media activity

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical study using machine learning analysis on ABCD data to identify modifiable factors related to cognitive functioning

FT_EC7_reason: Screen media activity is a predictor (independent variable) of cognitive functioning (outcome), not a dependent variable. FT-EC7 criteria (a) requires smartphone/SM to be dependent variable in every analysis, which is not the case here.
notes: ML framework includes screen media activity as one of multiple predictors. E4 applies: ML coefficients count as extractable numeric evidence.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzed prospective cohort data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study with N=11,025 children ages 9-10

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures specific screen time modalities: total screen time, social networking, texting, and watching/streaming television/movies. Per abstract: 'each additional hour of social networking (aOR 1.62, 95% CI 1.18-2.22), texting (aOR 1.40, 95% CI 1.08-1.82), and watching/streaming television shows/movies (aOR 1.39, 95% CI 1.14-1.69)'

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression analyses conducted to estimate associations between baseline screen time (exposure) and binge-eating disorder at one-year follow-up (outcome), with adjusted odds ratios reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Adjusted odds ratios (aOR) and 95% CIs reported for each screen time modality: total screen time (aOR 1.11 per hour, 95% CI 1.05-1.18); social networking (aOR 1.62, 95% CI 1.18-2.22); texting (aOR 1.40, 95% CI 1.08-1.82); TV/streaming (aOR 1.39, 95% CI 1.14-1.69)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective cohort study analyzing baseline screen time and one-year follow-up binge-eating disorder outcomes from ABCD Study

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed journal article (The International Journal of Eating Disorders, 2021)

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is the independent variable (predictor) and binge-eating disorder is the dependent variable (outcome). FT-EC7 requires screen/SM to be the dependent variable, which is not the case. Additionally, the outcome is a non-SM clinical variable (binge-eating disorder), so FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Prospective study with clear directionality: baseline screen time exposure predicts follow-up eating disorder outcome.
